2026-03-20T21:05:44.5109895Z {"timestamp":"2026-03-20T21:04:34.640058436Z","direction":"IN","type":"RESPONSE","server_id":"github","payload":{"id":1,"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"content":[{"text":"{\"number\":57314,\"title\":\"[QUERY] Why does `System.ClientModel` exist?\",\"body\":\"### Library name and version\\n\\nSystem.ClientModel 1.10.0\\n\\n### Query/Question\\n\\nEvery time I read about the features of `System.ClientModel`, I get more and more confused.\\n\\nI\\u0026#39;ve been using raw `HttpClient` (with `IHttpClientFactory` for a long time). More recently, I\\u0026#39;ve started using the `kiota` libraries along with openapi specs for as many APIs as we can. `kiota` comes with its own share of abstractions and \\u0026#34;pipeline\\u0026#34;-like extensions (via `DelegatingHandlers`) for various things. Then, even more recently, we started leveraging the `Microsoft.Extensions.ServiceDiscovery` library (initially part of Aspire) so that we could have a consistent means of specifying service urls in configuration and easily configure them, as well as to integrate with Aspire itself which relies heavily on the pattern.\\n\\nThen comes `System.ClientModel` and throws a MASSIVE wrench at everything we\\u0026#39;ve been using basically...\\n\\n- It (very recently) defined its own DI+configuration experience, using a completely new configuration section with properties that do not at all match the previous `Services` section defined by service discovery. This now means we have 2 distinct (and conflicting) mechanisms to represent our services in configuration\\n- It declares various abstractions around authentication, transport, etc that are _very_ similar to some of the abstractions defined in `kiota`, which makes everything confusing and redundant, especially if you want to use _both_\\n- It invents its own completely custom \\u0026#34;retry\\u0026#34; thing, when we already have _extensive_ Polly integration with `HttpClient`\\n- It comes up with this crazy \\u0026#34;configuration extension\\u0026#34; thing for declaring \\u0026#34;reusable variables\\u0026#34; in config, instead of pushing the `Extensions.Configuration` framework to support that natively or promoting something like [Workleap.Extensions.Configuration.Substitution](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Workleap.Extensions.Configuration.Substitution#readme-body-tab) or expanding upon it\\n- It is called `System.ClientModel` (super generic), yet it sits in the Azure SDK repo (WTH) and has \\u0026#34;cloud\\u0026#34; mentioned all over its documentation even though the abstractions themselves seem to be completely generic\\n\\nI just want to understand what Microsoft is doing here. You have 3 teams:\\n- Azure SDK team doing its own thing\\n- `kiota` team doing its own thing\\n- dotnet/aspnetcore/extensions team doing its own thing\\n\\nHow are we supposed to reconcile all this mess? Why don\\u0026#39;t you talk with each other to come up with a _common_ set of abstractions that work together for all purposes instead of each one creating their own thing in an incompatible manner?\\n\\nMaybe I\\u0026#39;m just missing something incredibly obvious here, but I\\u0026#39;m frustrated right now: there seems to be an opportunity to unify and simplify things around service calling/configuration here, and from my perspective your teams are dropping the ball and making an absolute mess of it.\\n\\nWhat is the purpose of this library exactly and why is it so custom compared to other abstractions in the ecosystem such as `kiota` and service discovery?\\n\\n@CarnaViire\\n@davidfowl \\n@baywet (I know you are not in the `kiota` team anymore but you must have the most context here)\\n\\nThoughts?\\n\\n\\n### Environment\\n\\nN/A\",\"state\":\"open\",\"html_url\":\"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/issues/57314\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"julealgon\",\"id\":461579,\"profile_url\":\"https://github.com/julealgon\",\"avatar_url\":\"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/461579?v=4\"},\"author_association\":\"NONE\",\"reactions\":{\"total_count\":1,\"+1\":0,\"-1\":0,\"laugh\":0,\"confused\":0,\"heart\":0,\"hooray\":0,\"rocket\":0,\"eyes\":1},\"created_at\":\"2026-03-20T21:02:28Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-03-20T21:02:28Z\"}","type":"text"}]}}}

2026-03-20T21:05:44.5236940Z {"timestamp":"2026-03-20T21:04:43.799022704Z","direction":"IN","type":"RESPONSE","server_id":"github","payload":{"id":1,"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"content":[{"text":"{\"issues\":[{\"number\":57314,\"title\":\"[QUERY] Why does `System.ClientModel` exist?\",\"body\":\"### Library name and version\\n\\nSystem.ClientModel 1.10.0\\n\\n### Query/Question\\n\\nEvery time I read about the features of `System.ClientModel`, I get more and more confused.\\n\\nI\\u0026#39;ve been using raw `HttpClient` (with `IHttpClientFactory` for a long time). More recently, I\\u0026#39;ve started using the `kiota` libraries along with openapi specs for as many APIs as we can. `kiota` comes with its own share of abstractions and \\u0026#34;pipeline\\u0026#34;-like extensions (via `DelegatingHandlers`) for various things. Then, even more recently, we started leveraging the `Microsoft.Extensions.ServiceDiscovery` library (initially part of Aspire) so that we could have a consistent means of specifying service urls in configuration and easily configure them, as well as to integrate with Aspire itself which relies heavily on the pattern.\\n\\nThen comes `System.ClientModel` and throws a MASSIVE wrench at everything we\\u0026#39;ve been using basically...\\n\\n- It (very recently) defined its own DI+configuration experience, using a completely new configuration section with properties that do not at all match the previous `Services` section defined by service discovery. This now means we have 2 distinct (and conflicting) mechanisms to represent our services in configuration\\n- It declares various abstractions around authentication, transport, etc that are _very_ similar to some of the abstractions defined in `kiota`, which makes everything confusing and redundant, especially if you want to use _both_\\n- It invents its own completely custom \\u0026#34;retry\\u0026#34; thing, when we already have _extensive_ Polly integration with `HttpClient`\\n- It comes up with this crazy \\u0026#34;configuration extension\\u0026#34; thing for declaring \\u0026#34;reusable variables\\u0026#34; in config, instead of pushing the `Extensions.Configuration` framework to support that natively or promoting something like [Workleap.Extensions.Configuration.Substitution](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Workleap.Extensions.Configuration.Substitution#readme-body-tab) or expanding upon it\\n- It is called `System.ClientModel` (super generic), yet it sits in the Azure SDK repo (WTH) and has \\u0026#34;cloud\\u0026#34; mentioned all over its documentation even though the abstractions themselves seem to be completely generic\\n\\nI just want to understand what Microsoft is doing here. You have 3 teams:\\n- Azure SDK team doing its own thing\\n- `kiota` team doing its own thing\\n- dotnet/aspnetcore/extensions team doing its own thing\\n\\nHow are we supposed to reconcile all this mess? Why don\\u0026#39;t you talk with each other to come up with a _common_ set of abstractions that work together for all purposes instead of each one creating their own thing in an incompatible manner?\\n\\nMaybe I\\u0026#39;m just missing something incredibly obvious here, but I\\u0026#39;m frustrated right now: there seems to be an opportunity to unify and simplify things around service calling/configuration here, and from my perspective your teams are dropping the ball and making an absolute mess of it.\\n\\nWhat is the purpose of this library exactly and why is it so custom compared to other abstractions in the ecosystem such as `kiota` and service discovery?\\n\\n@CarnaViire\\n@davidfowl \\n@baywet (I know you are not in the `kiota` team anymore but you must have the most context here)\\n\\nThoughts?\\n\\n\\n### Environment\\n\\nN/A\",\"state\":\"OPEN\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"julealgon\"},\"created_at\":\"2026-03-20T21:02:28Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-03-20T21:02:28Z\"},{\"number\":57311,\"title\":\"Revert temporary ProjectReferences to PackageReferences after Azure.Core 1.52 ships\",\"body\":\"After Azure.Core 1.52 ships with the Identity types included, the following temporary ProjectReference workarounds should be reverted back to PackageReferences:\\n\\n- \\\\sdk/identity/Azure.Identity.Broker/src/Azure.Identity.Broker.csproj\\\\\\n- \\\\sdk/confidentialledger/Azure.Security.CodeTransparency/tests/Azure.Security.CodeTransparency.Tests.csproj\\\\\\n- (if applicable)\\n\\nThese were changed to avoid CS0433 type conflicts during development when local Azure.Core contained Identity types but NuGet Azure.Identity still had the original types.\\n\\nParent: #55491\",\"state\":\"OPEN\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"m-nash\"},\"labels\":[\"Client\",\"Azure.Core\"],\"created_at\":\"2026-03-20T18:58:37Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-03-20T18:58:52Z\"},{\"number\":57308,\"title\":\"Disable api-version automation in validation pipeline\",\"body\":\"Fundamentally .NET doesn\\u0026#39;t support generating a different api-version than what is specified in tspconfig.yaml, so we should just disable this feature. This is because we have guardrails in place to ensure that the generated code is reproducible based on the tspconfig.yaml. The api-version passed via release planner is not stored anywhere. We don\\u0026#39;t support having generated code that doesn\\u0026#39;t match the commit SHA.\\n\\nhttps://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/eng/scripts/Invoke-GenerateAndBuildV2.ps1#L252-L258\\n\\nWe should just log a warning if it is specified.\",\"state\":\"OPEN\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"JoshLove-msft\"},\"labels\":[\"Client\",\"EngSys\"],\"created_at\":\"2026-03-20T17:48:06Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-03-20T18:56:49Z\"},{\"number\":57303,\"title\":\"The generated code should use ToSerialString() to construct a request\",\"body\":\"When serializing an enum to a string, the generated code should call ToSerialString.\\nHowever, when an enum is used as a path parameter (for example, as shown below):\\n\\n``` js\\n/**\\n * Regenerates either the primary or secondary admin API key. You can only regenerate one key at a time.\\n */\\n#suppress \\u0026#34;@azure-tools/typespec-azure-resource-manager/arm-resource-operation\\u0026#34; \\u0026#34;FIXME: Update justification, follow aka.ms/tsp/conversion-fix for details\\u0026#34;\\n@autoRoute\\n@post\\nop privateLinkResourcesRegenerateCustomization(\\n  ...ApiVersionParameter,\\n  ...SubscriptionIdParameter,\\n  ...CommonTypes.ResourceGroupNameParameter,\\n\\n  /** The name of the Azure AI Search service associated with the specified resource group. */\\n  @path\\n  @segment(\\u0026#34;searchServices\\u0026#34;)\\n  @key\\n  @pattern(\\u0026#34;^(?=.{2,60}$)[a-z0-9][a-z0-9]+(-[a-z0-9]+)*$\\u0026#34;)\\n  searchServiceName: string,\\n\\n  /**\\n   * Specifies which key to regenerate. Valid values include \\u0026#39;primary\\u0026#39; and \\u0026#39;secondary\\u0026#39;.\\n   */\\n  @path\\n  keyKind: AdminKeyKind,\\n\\n  #suppress \\u0026#34;@azure-tools/typespec-azure-core/documentation-required\\u0026#34; \\u0026#34;FIXME: Update justification, follow aka.ms/tsp/conversion-fix for details\\u0026#34;\\n  body: void,\\n  #suppress \\u0026#34;@azure-tools/typespec-azure-core/documentation-required\\u0026#34; \\u0026#34;FIXME: Update justification, follow aka.ms/tsp/conversion-fix for details\\u0026#34;\\n  params?: `search-management-request-options`,\\n): ArmResponse | CloudError;\\n```\\n\\nThe generated code currently uses ToString() instead of ToSerialString() when constructing the request.\\nFor example, the generated code for the definition above is:\\n\\n``` .net\\n        public virtual Response RegenerateAdminKey(SearchServiceAdminKeyKind keyKind, SearchManagementRequestOptions searchManagementRequestOptions = default, CancellationToken cancellationToken = default)\\n        {\\n            using DiagnosticScope scope = _adminKeysClientDiagnostics.CreateScope(\\u0026#34;SearchServiceResource.RegenerateAdminKey\\u0026#34;);\\n            scope.Start();\\n            try\\n            {\\n                RequestContext context = new RequestContext\\n                {\\n                    CancellationToken = cancellationToken\\n                };\\n                HttpMessage message = _adminKeysRestClient.CreateRegenerateAdminKeyRequest(Guid.Parse(Id.SubscriptionId), Id.ResourceGroupName, Id.Name, keyKind.ToString(), default, context);\\n                Response result = Pipeline.ProcessMessage(message, context);\\n                Response response = Response.FromValue(SearchServiceAdminKeyResult.FromResponse(result), result);\\n                if (response.Value == null)\\n                {\\n                    throw new RequestFailedException(response.GetRawResponse());\\n                }\\n                return response;\\n            }\\n            catch (Exception e)\\n            {\\n                scope.Failed(e);\\n                throw;\\n            }\\n        }\\n```\\n\\nThe expected behavior is to use ToSerialString() when serializing the enum value for the request. The correct generated code should be:\\n\\n``` .net\\n        public virtual Response RegenerateAdminKey(SearchServiceAdminKeyKind keyKind, SearchManagementRequestOptions searchManagementRequestOptions = default, CancellationToken cancellationToken = default)\\n        {\\n            using DiagnosticScope scope = _adminKeysClientDiagnostics.CreateScope(\\u0026#34;SearchServiceResource.RegenerateAdminKey\\u0026#34;);\\n            scope.Start();\\n            try\\n            {\\n                RequestContext context = new RequestContext\\n                {\\n                    CancellationToken = cancellationToken\\n                };\\n                HttpMessage message = _adminKeysRestClient.CreateRegenerateAdminKeyRequest(Guid.Parse(Id.SubscriptionId), Id.ResourceGroupName, Id.Name, keyKind.ToSerialString(), default, context);\\n                Response result = Pipeline.ProcessMessage(message, context);\\n                Response response = Response.FromValue(SearchServiceAdminKeyResult.FromResponse(result), result);\\n                if (response.Value == null)\\n                {\\n                    throw new RequestFailedException(response.GetRawResponse());\\n                }\\n                return response;\\n            }\\n            catch (Exception e)\\n            {\\n                scope.Failed(e);\\n                throw;\\n            }\\n        }\\n```\",\"state\":\"OPEN\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"ArthurMa1978\"},\"labels\":[\"Mgmt\",\"CodeGen\"],\"created_at\":\"2026-03-20T16:48:11Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-03-20T16:48:11Z\"},{\"number\":57293,\"title\":\"[Mgmt Generator] Missing WireV3Options for ManagedServiceIdentity serialization\",\"body\":\"## Description\\n\\nThe mgmt generator does not emit `WireV3Options` for `ManagedServiceIdentity` serialization. This causes incorrect wire format for the `ManagedServiceIdentityType` value when `@armCommonTypesVersion` is v3, v5, or v6.\\n\\n## Root Cause\\n\\nThe CommonTypes `ManagedServiceIdentityType` union has **different wire values per version**:\\n\\n```typespec\\nunion ManagedServiceIdentityType {\\n  \\u0026#34;None\\u0026#34;,\\n  \\u0026#34;SystemAssigned\\u0026#34;,\\n  \\u0026#34;UserAssigned\\u0026#34;,\\n\\n  @added(Versions.v3) @removed(Versions.v4) @added(Versions.v5)\\n  \\u0026#34;SystemAssigned,UserAssigned\\u0026#34;,          // no space — v3, v5, v6\\n\\n  @added(Versions.v4) @removed(Versions.v5)\\n  \\u0026#34;SystemAssigned, UserAssigned\\u0026#34;,         // with space — v4 only\\n\\n  string,\\n}\\n```\\n\\nThe C# ARM SDK\\u0026#39;s `ManagedServiceIdentityType.SystemAssignedUserAssigned` has internal value `\\u0026#34;SystemAssigned, UserAssigned\\u0026#34;` (**v4 format, with space**). The ARM SDK\\u0026#39;s `ManagedServiceIdentity.Serialization.cs` supports a `\\u0026#34;|v3\\u0026#34;` format suffix to switch to the no-space wire format:\\n\\n- `\\u0026#34;W|v3\\u0026#34;` → writes `\\u0026#34;SystemAssigned,UserAssigned\\u0026#34;` (no space, correct for v3/v5/v6)\\n- `\\u0026#34;W\\u0026#34;` → writes `ToString()` = `\\u0026#34;SystemAssigned, UserAssigned\\u0026#34;` (with space, correct for v4)\\n\\n## How the old autorest generator detected this\\n\\nThe old autorest generator (`autorest.csharp`) uses `IsManagedServiceIdentityV3` in `SerializationBuilder.cs`:\\n\\n```csharp\\nprivate static bool IsManagedServiceIdentityV3(InputType schema, CSharpType type)\\n{\\n    // 1. Check: C# type maps to the system ManagedServiceIdentity\\n    if (type is not { IsFrameworkType: false, Implementation: SystemObjectType systemObjectType }\\n        || systemObjectType.SystemType != typeof(ManagedServiceIdentity)\\n        || schema is not InputModelType objectSchema)\\n        return false;\\n\\n    // 2. Check: the INPUT schema\\u0026#39;s \\u0026#34;type\\u0026#34; property enum contains \\u0026#34;SystemAssigned,UserAssigned\\u0026#34; (no space)\\n    foreach (var m in objectSchema.GetSelfAndBaseModels())\\n        foreach (var property in m.Properties)\\n            if (property is { SerializedName: \\u0026#34;type\\u0026#34;, Type: InputEnumType choice }\\n                \\u0026amp;\\u0026amp; choice.Values.Any(c =\\u0026gt; c.Value.ToString() == \\u0026#34;SystemAssigned,UserAssigned\\u0026#34;))\\n                return true;\\n\\n    return false;\\n}\\n```\\n\\n**Key insight:** It does NOT check `@armCommonTypesVersion` directly. Instead it inspects the **input schema** (the original spec model before type mapping) and checks if the `type` property\\u0026#39;s enum contains the no-space value `\\u0026#34;SystemAssigned,UserAssigned\\u0026#34;`. This works because:\\n- v3/v5/v6 specs define `\\u0026#34;SystemAssigned,UserAssigned\\u0026#34;` (no space) → matches → `WireV3Options`\\n- v4 specs define `\\u0026#34;SystemAssigned, UserAssigned\\u0026#34;` (with space) → doesn\\u0026#39;t match → plain `WireOptions`\\n\\nWhen matched, autorest uses `WireV3Options` / `WireV3Options` for both serialization and deserialization of `ManagedServiceIdentity`. See `JsonCodeWriterExtensions.cs:171` and `JsonSerializationMethodsBuilder.cs:445`.\\n\\n## Version → WireOptions mapping\\n\\n| `@armCommonTypesVersion` | Identity type wire value | Options needed |\\n|---|---|---|\\n| v3 | `\\u0026#34;SystemAssigned,UserAssigned\\u0026#34;` (no space) | `WireV3Options` (`\\u0026#34;W\\\\|v3\\u0026#34;`) |\\n| v4 | `\\u0026#34;SystemAssigned, UserAssigned\\u0026#34;` (with space) | `WireOptions` (`\\u0026#34;W\\u0026#34;`) — current default |\\n| v5 | `\\u0026#34;SystemAssigned,UserAssigned\\u0026#34;` (no space) | `WireV3Options` (`\\u0026#34;W\\\\|v3\\u0026#34;`) |\\n| v6 | `\\u0026#34;SystemAssigned,UserAssigned\\u0026#34;` (no space) | `WireV3Options` (`\\u0026#34;W\\\\|v3\\u0026#34;`) |\\n\\n## Repro\\n\\nAlready reproducible in the existing mgmt test project — no new TypeSpec needed.\\n\\nThe test project uses `@armCommonTypesVersion(v5)` and `Foo` has `...ManagedServiceIdentityV4Property`.\\n\\n**Generated code** (`FooData.Serialization.cs` line 115):\\n```csharp\\n// Current (bug): uses plain options\\n((IJsonModel)Identity).Write(writer, options);\\n```\\n\\n**Expected:**\\n```csharp\\n// Should use WireV3Options for v3/v5/v6\\n((IJsonModel)Identity).Write(writer, ModelSerializationExtensions.WireV3Options);\\n```\\n\\n## Suggested Fix\\n\\nThe new mgmt emitter/generator should replicate autorest\\u0026#39;s detection logic:\\n\\n1. When the output C# type is `ManagedServiceIdentity`, inspect the **input schema** (original spec model)\\n2. Check if the input model\\u0026#39;s `type` property has an enum value `\\u0026#34;SystemAssigned,UserAssigned\\u0026#34;` (no space)\\n3. If yes, generate `WireV3Options = new ModelReaderWriterOptions(\\u0026#34;W|v3\\u0026#34;)` in `ModelSerializationExtensions` and use it for serialization/deserialization of that property\\n4. If no (v4 format with space), use the default `WireOptions`\\n\\nAlternatively, the emitter can check the `@armCommonTypesVersion` via `getArmCommonTypesVersion(program, entity)` (exported from `@azure-tools/typespec-azure-resource-manager`) and emit `WireV3Options` for v3/v5/v6.\\n\\n## Impact\\n\\n- Breaks wire compatibility: sends `\\u0026#34;SystemAssigned, UserAssigned\\u0026#34;` (with space) instead of `\\u0026#34;SystemAssigned,UserAssigned\\u0026#34;` (no space) for v3/v5/v6 specs\\n- Causes test recording mismatches (8 failures in Storage MPG migration PR #56896)\\n\\n## Related\\n\\n- Storage MPG migration PR: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/pull/56896\\n- Autorest detection: `autorest.csharp/src/AutoRest.CSharp/Common/Output/Builders/SerializationBuilder.cs` — `IsManagedServiceIdentityV3`\\n- ARM SDK v3 logic: `sdk/resourcemanager/Azure.ResourceManager/src/Common/Custom/Models/ManagedServiceIdentity.Serialization.cs`\\n\",\"state\":\"OPEN\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"live1206\"},\"labels\":[\"Mgmt\",\"CodeGen\"],\"comments\":2,\"created_at\":\"2026-03-20T10:25:46Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-03-20T10:45:34Z\"},{\"number\":57287,\"title\":\"[Generator Bug] @@alternateType to armResourceType emits WriteObjectValue instead of WriteStringValue\",\"body\":\"## Description\\n\\nWhen `@@alternateType` maps a property to `Azure.Core.armResourceType` (C# `ResourceType`), the generator emits `WriteObjectValue(ResourceType, options)` for serialization. But `WriteObjectValue` has no case for `ResourceType` (which is a `readonly struct` wrapping a string), so it falls through to the `default` case and throws:\\n\\n```\\nSystem.NotSupportedException: Not supported type Azure.Core.ResourceType\\n```\\n\\nThis also affects deserialization — the generator emits `ResourceType.DeserializeResourceType()` which doesn\\u0026#39;t exist as a public method.\\n\\n## Affected Model (Storage MPG Migration)\\n\\n- `StorageAccountNameAvailabilityContent` — spec has `type: \\u0026#34;Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts\\u0026#34;` (constant string), mapped via:\\n```typespec\\n@@alternateType(StorageAccountCheckNameAvailabilityParameters.type, Azure.Core.armResourceType, \\u0026#34;csharp\\u0026#34;);\\n```\\n\\n## Impact\\n\\nThis blocks **230 out of 287 tests** in the Storage SDK because nearly every test calls `CheckStorageAccountNameAvailability` during setup.\\n\\n## Current Workaround\\n\\nCustom `[CodeGenSerialization]` hooks for both directions:\\n\\n```csharp\\n[CodeGenSerialization(nameof(ResourceType),\\n    DeserializationValueHook = nameof(DeserializeResourceType),\\n    SerializationValueHook = nameof(SerializeResourceType))]\\npublic partial class StorageAccountNameAvailabilityContent\\n{\\n    private static void SerializeResourceType(Utf8JsonWriter writer, ResourceType resourceType)\\n    {\\n        writer.WriteStringValue(resourceType.ToString());\\n    }\\n\\n    private static void DeserializeResourceType(JsonProperty property, ref ResourceType resourceType)\\n    {\\n        resourceType = new ResourceType(property.Value.GetString());\\n    }\\n}\\n```\\n\\n**Note:** The `SerializationValueHook` requires regeneration to take effect. The generated file still has `WriteObjectValue` until regenerated.\\n\\n## Comparison with Native Usage\\n\\nWhen `armResourceType` is used as a **native property type** (not via `@@alternateType`), the generator correctly emits `WriteStringValue(ResourceType)`. The bug is specific to the `@@alternateType` code path.\\n\\n**Works (native):** `testresourcetype.tsp`\\n```typespec\\nmodel ResourceTypeTestProperties {\\n  optionalResourceType?: Azure.Core.armResourceType;  // generates WriteStringValue ✓\\n}\\n```\\n\\n**Broken (alternateType):** `client.tsp`\\n```typespec\\n@@alternateType(SomeModel.type, Azure.Core.armResourceType, \\u0026#34;csharp\\u0026#34;);  // generates WriteObjectValue ✗\\n```\\n\\n## Related PR\\n\\n- SDK PR: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/pull/56896\\n\",\"state\":\"OPEN\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"live1206\"},\"labels\":[\"Mgmt\",\"bug\",\"CodeGen\"],\"comments\":2,\"created_at\":\"2026-03-20T08:33:49Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-03-20T16:07:28Z\"},{\"number\":57282,\"title\":\"[Generator Bug] Incorrect deserialization of SubResource collections in NspAccessRuleProperties\",\"body\":\"## Description\\n\\nThe generated deserialization code for `NspAccessRuleProperties.Subscriptions` (typed as `IReadOnlyList`) does not correctly deserialize `SubResource` items from the JSON array. A custom deserialization hook via `[CodeGenSerialization]` is required as a workaround.\\n\\n## Affected Model\\n\\n- `NspAccessRuleProperties` (`sdk/storage/Azure.ResourceManager.Storage/src/Generated/Models/NspAccessRuleProperties.cs`)\\n\\n## Current Workaround\\n\\nA `[CodeGenSerialization]` attribute with a custom deserialization hook is used in customization code:\\n\\n```csharp\\n[CodeGenSerialization(nameof(Subscriptions), DeserializationValueHook = nameof(DeserializeSubscriptionsList))]\\npublic partial class NspAccessRuleProperties\\n{\\n    internal static void DeserializeSubscriptionsList(JsonProperty property, ref IReadOnlyList subscriptions)\\n    {\\n        // Manual deserialization using ModelReaderWriter.Read()\\n    }\\n}\\n```\\n\\n## Expected Behavior\\n\\nThe generator should correctly deserialize `SubResource` collection properties without requiring custom hooks.\\n\\n## Related PR\\n\\n- SDK PR: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/pull/56896\\n\",\"state\":\"OPEN\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"live1206\"},\"labels\":[\"Mgmt\",\"CodeGen\"],\"comments\":2,\"created_at\":\"2026-03-20T06:24:05Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-03-20T08:16:28Z\"},{\"number\":57281,\"title\":\"[Generator Bug] Generated serialization references undeclared \\u0026#39;additionalBinaryDataProperties0\\u0026#39; variable\",\"body\":\"## Description\\n\\nWhen generating models that inherit from a base type with additional binary data properties, the generated serialization code references a variable `additionalBinaryDataProperties0` that is never declared in the generated model class.\\n\\n## Affected Models (Storage MPG Migration)\\n\\n- `ListContainerItem` (`sdk/storage/Azure.ResourceManager.Storage/src/Generated/Models/ListContainerItem.Serialization.cs`)\\n- `FileShareItem` (`sdk/storage/Azure.ResourceManager.Storage/src/Generated/Models/FileShareItem.Serialization.cs`)\\n\\n## Current Workaround\\n\\nA static field is added in customization code to provide the missing variable:\\n\\n```csharp\\n[ThreadStatic]\\nprivate static IDictionary s_additionalBinaryDataProperties0;\\nprivate static IDictionary additionalBinaryDataProperties0\\n    =\\u0026gt; s_additionalBinaryDataProperties0 ??= new Dictionary();\\n```\\n\\nNote: This workaround uses `[ThreadStatic]` which does not preserve additional properties per-instance.\\n\\n## Expected Behavior\\n\\nThe generator should declare `additionalBinaryDataProperties0` as a local variable in the deserialization method, or use the existing `_additionalBinaryDataProperties` field from the model.\\n\\n## Related PR\\n\\n- SDK PR: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/pull/56896\\n\",\"state\":\"OPEN\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"live1206\"},\"labels\":[\"Mgmt\",\"CodeGen\"],\"comments\":1,\"created_at\":\"2026-03-20T06:23:46Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-03-20T08:16:35Z\"},{\"number\":57275,\"title\":\"SDK Validation - .NET should only be required for the new mgmt csharp emitter, not for swagger-based generation\",\"body\":\"## Problem\\n\\nIn TypeSpec migration PRs (e.g., [Azure/azure-rest-api-specs#41000](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/pull/41000)), `SDK Validation - .NET` fails during swagger-based SDK generation and causes `SDK Validation Status` to be marked as **required and failed**, blocking the PR from merging.\\n\\n### Specific Example\\n\\nIn [azure-rest-api-specs#41000](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/pull/41000) (TypeSpec conversion for reservations):\\n- The PR adds TypeSpec files for the reservations service\\n- The `tspconfig.yaml` has **no C# emitter configuration** — only Python, Java, JS, and Go emitters are configured\\n- The .NET SDK (`Azure.ResourceManager.Reservations`) is still swagger-based (uses `autorest.md`, no `tsp-location.yaml`)\\n- SDK Validation - .NET runs and generates from swagger, which **fails**\\n- Because the PR has TypeSpec files, `isSpecGenSdkCheckRequired` is set to `true` for .NET management plane\\n- This causes `SDK Validation Status` to fail and block the PR\\n\\nSee comment: https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/pull/41000#issuecomment-4073647992\\n\\n## Root Cause\\n\\nThe `isSpecGenSdkCheckRequired` determination in the spec repo\\u0026#39;s `spec-gen-sdk-runner` ([`getRequiredSettingValue()`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/blob/main/eng/tools/spec-gen-sdk-runner/src/command-helpers.ts)) is **too broad** for .NET:\\n\\n```typescript\\n// eng/tools/spec-gen-sdk-runner/src/command-helpers.ts\\nexport function getRequiredSettingValue(\\n  hasManagementPlaneSpecs: boolean,\\n  hasTypeSpecProjects: boolean,\\n  sdkName: SdkName,\\n): boolean {\\n  // If the SDK is azure-sdk-for-net, return false if there are no TypeSpec projects.\\n  if (sdkName === \\u0026#34;azure-sdk-for-net\\u0026#34; \\u0026amp;\\u0026amp; !hasTypeSpecProjects) {\\n    return false;\\n  }\\n  if (hasManagementPlaneSpecs) {\\n    return SpecGenSdkRequiredSettings[sdkName].managementPlane; // true for .NET\\n  }\\n  ...\\n}\\n```\\n\\nFor .NET, the check is required when `hasTypeSpecProjects = true` AND the spec is management plane. But `hasTypeSpecProjects` is determined by whether the PR has **any** TypeSpec files — it does **not** check if the TypeSpec project has a C# emitter configured (`@azure-tools/typespec-csharp`, `@azure-typespec/http-client-csharp`, or `@azure-typespec/http-client-csharp-mgmt`).\\n\\nFor the reservations PR:\\n- `hasTypeSpecProjects = true` (PR has TypeSpec files)\\n- `hasManagementPlaneSpecs = true` (management plane service)\\n- `SpecGenSdkRequiredSettings[\\u0026#34;azure-sdk-for-net\\u0026#34;].managementPlane = true`\\n- Result: `isSpecGenSdkCheckRequired = true` — **incorrect** because the .NET SDK has no TypeSpec generation path\\n\\n## Expected Behavior\\n\\n`SDK Validation - .NET` should only be **required** when the TypeSpec project uses the **new mgmt csharp emitter** (`@azure-typespec/http-client-csharp-mgmt`).\\n\\nFor management plane services NOT using the new mgmt emitter (still swagger-based or using older emitters), SDK Validation - .NET should be **optional** (not blocking).\\n\\n## Proposed Fix\\n\\nThe `isSpecGenSdkCheckRequired` determination should be moved to the SDK repo\\u0026#39;s generation script, which already has the logic to detect the new mgmt emitter.\\n\\nIn [`Invoke-GenerateAndBuildV2.ps1`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/eng/scripts/Invoke-GenerateAndBuildV2.ps1), the function `Test-MgmtSdkUsingNewGenerator` (lines 48-70) already correctly detects whether a package uses the new mgmt csharp emitter:\\n\\n```powershell\\nfunction Test-MgmtSdkUsingNewGenerator {\\n    param(\\n        [string]$serviceType,\\n        [string]$tspConfigFile,\\n        [string]$sdkProjectFolder\\n    )\\n    if ($serviceType -ne \\u0026#39;resource-manager\\u0026#39;) { return $false }\\n    # checks tspconfig.yaml for @azure-typespec/http-client-csharp-mgmt\\n    # checks tsp-location.yaml for mgmt emitter package json path\\n    ...\\n}\\n```\\n\\n### Suggested approach:\\n1. **In the SDK repo** (`Invoke-GenerateAndBu
2026-03-20T21:05:44.5237543Z ...
